coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric and visual determination of copper ions with fluorescent nanohybrids of semiconducting polymer nanoparticles and carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Fabao
Zhu, Mengjun
Liu, Yizhang
Sun, Junyong
Gao, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Developing nanohybrid composition based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) for ratiometric detection of copper ions is highly appealing. Herein, a ratiometric sensing platform (GCDs@RSPN) for copper ions detection has been developed by loaded green fluorescence carbon dots (GCDs) on the surface of red emission semiconducting polymer nanoparticles (RSPN) through electrostatic adsorption. The GCDs, featuring abundant amino groups, can selectively bind copper ions to induce the photoinduced electron transfer, leading to fluorescence quenching. A good linearity within the range of 0–100 μM is obtained, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.577 μM by using obtained GCDs@RSPN as ratiometric probe to detect copper ion. Moreover, the paper-based sensor derived from GCDs@RSPN was successfully applied for the visual detection of Cu2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanohybrids
Ratiometric probe
Copper ions
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for fluorescence detection of Cu2+.
Procedure for the real water samples.
Preparation of paper-based sensor for visual detection of Cu2+.
Sensing strategy of the ratiometric probe.
Response of the fluorescence of GCDs@RSPN to Cu2+.
Application for detection in real samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Average diameter of GCDs measured by DLS. Inset: TEM and high-resolution TEM image of GCDs. (B) XRD pattern of GCDs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of GCDs. (D) Average diameter of RSPN measured by DLS. Inset: TEM image of RSPN. (E) Average diameter of GCDs@RSPN measured by DLS. Inset: TEM image of GCDs@RSPN. (F) Zeta potential of RSPN (a), GCDs (b) and GCDs@RSPN (c).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the GCDs@RSPN with increasing Cu2+ ranged from 0 to 200 μM. (B) Fluorescence photos of the probe solution taken under a UV lamp. (C) Plot of the relative intensity (I501/I625) versus the concentrations of Cu2+. Inset: the linear relationship between the logarithm of intensity ratio (I501/I625) and the concentrations of Cu2+. (D) Fluorescence response of GCDs@RSPN for different metal ions and other biologically relevant analytes in the aqueous solution of PB (50 mM, pH 8.00). (a: without EDTA, b: with 100 μM EDTA, 1. Blank, 2. Na+, 3. K+, 4. Mn2+, 5. Ca2+, 6. Zr4+, 7. Cd2+, 8. Pb2+ 9. Ba2+, 10. Hg2+, 11. Al3+, 12. Zn2+, 13. Ag+, 14. Mg2+, 15. Fe3+, 16. Ni2+, 17. DL - valine, 18. Sarcosine, 19. L-Proline, 20. Citrulline, 21. glucose, 22. Threonine, 23. L-Arginine, 24. Cu2+, 25. Co2+) (E) Fluorescence responses of the GCDs at different concentrations of Cu2+ from 0 to 200 μM. (F) Plot of fluorescence intensity ratio at 501 nm versus the concentrations of Cu2+. Inset: the linear relationship between logarithm of fluorescence intensity of GCDs and Cu2+ concentrations.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime of GCDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+. (B) Absorption (a, b), excitation (c, d) and emission spectra (e, f) of GCDs before (a, c, e) and after (b, d, f) the addition of Cu2+.

(A)Visual detection of Cu2+ by using filter paper strips smeared with GCDs@RSPN probes. The photos were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) The ratio of the green channel to the red channel (G/R) varies with the concentration of Cu2+. Inset: Plot of test paper color change (G/R) versus the concentration of Cu2+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of GCDs@RSPN for detecting Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric and visual determination of copper ions with fluorescent nanohybrids of semiconducting polymer nanoparticles and carbon dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Laboratory of Functionalized Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing, Laboratory of Biosensing and Bioimaging, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241002, China (Y. Liu).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent nanohybrids of carbon dots and semiconducting polymer nanoparticles were used for ratiometric and visual determination of of copper ions.

Developing nanohybrid composition based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) for ratiometric detection of copper ions is highly appealing. Herein, a ratiometric sensing platform (GCDs@RSPN) for copper ions detection has been developed by loaded green fluorescence carbon dots (GCDs) on the surface of red emission semiconducting polymer nanoparticles (RSPN) through electrostatic adsorption. The GCDs, featuring abundant amino groups, can selectively bind copper ions to induce the photoinduced electron transfer, leading to fluorescence quenching. A good linearity within the range of 0–100 μM is obtained, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.577 μM by using obtained GCDs@RSPN as ratiometric probe to detect copper ion. Moreover, the paper-based sensor derived from GCDs@RSPN was successfully applied for the visual detection of Cu2+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper, as an essential micronutrients, plays an important role in the metabolism of human body, such as bone formation, enzyme cofactors, cell respiration, connective tissue development and other physiological processes [1–3]. However, high levels of copper are harmful to biological organisms. Excessive intake of copper will cause the body's dysfunction, including slow down the metabolism, and even trigger anemia, pancytopenia, Wilson's disease, Alzheimer's disease and other diseases [4,5]. The rapid development of modern industry and agriculture has led to an excess of copper ions entering the environment and contaminating water and soil [6]. Therefore, monitoring copper ion levels in food and environmental water samples is essential for human health and environmental protection [7]. So far, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) and inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) are the most commonly used methods to determine the copper content in real samples [8,9]. However, those methods are time consuming and expensive equipment, limiting their application in large scales. Fluorescent sensors, featuring advantages such as simplicity, fast response, high selectivity, high sensitivity and non-invasiveness, have attracted much attention [10–12]. Therefore, constructing high-performance fluorescent probes for copper ion detection is highly desired.

Recently, fluorescent methods base on different strategies have been developed for Cu2+ sensing. Among them, those probes based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have drawn considerable attention own to their advantages, such as impressive water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, good photostability, low toxicity, etc [13,14]. Much effort has focused on modulating the surface state of CDs to improve the selectivity of the copper ion response [15–17]. Despite this progress, the majority of carbon dots-based probes rely on the intensity changes of single fluorescence peak for copper ion quantification, which can be affected by intensity fluctuations derived from instrumentation, probe concentration, excitation light source fluctuations and environmental conditions [18]. Ratiometric probes, derived from functionalized carbon dots, are able to overcome the above deficiencies to a certain extent by measuring the relative changes of two fluorescence peaks [19,20]. Apart from functionalized strategies, such as heteroatom doping and organic dye modification, it is also attractive to design novel hybrid nanocomposites between carbon dots and other nanostructured materials to achieve ratiometric sensing [21]. For instance, Shangguan’s group developed ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor by coating CDs on the surface of Rhodamine B-doped silica nanoparticles [22]. Therefore, it is of interest to find novel nanostructure materials and hybridize them with fluorescent carbon dots to construct ratiometric probes for copper ions detection.

Semiconducting polymer nanoparticles (SPNs), prepared from fluorescent conjugated polymer, have recently received considerable attention in the field of chemical sensing and biological imaging owning to their intrinsic merits, such as high brightness, tunable spectral properties, good photostability and non-blinking features [23,24]. In addition, due to the folding and intertwining of conjugated polymers, the SPNs possess clew-like nanostructure with high accessible surface area [25], which is suitable for fabrication of hybrid nanocomposites. However, the application of SPNs to fabricate hybrid nanocomposites is less exploited and mainly focused on photodynamic therapy [26]. Therefore, designing SPNs-based hybrid nanocomposites as ratiometric probes is still imperative.

In this study, we designed a nanohybrid sensing system for ratiometric detection of copper ion by means of a smart assembly of green fluorescent carbon dots (GCDs) with red emission polyfluorene-4,7-dithiophene-2,1,3-benzothiadiazole (PFDBT-5) polymer nanoparticles (RSPN). As shown in Scheme 1 , green fluorescent GCDs, prepared from polyethylenimine (PEI) and sunset yellow (SY) via hydrothermal method, display good fluorescence response for copper ion. The positively charged GCDs with smaller size were loaded on the surface of RSPN by electrostatic adsorption. The inherent properties of SPNs, including the “molecular wire” effect of the conjugated polymer and the clew-like structure, ensure satisfactory structural stability and signal conductivity of GCDs@RSPN nanoprobe. By employing green fluorescent GCDs as response units and RSPN as reference signals, ratiometric detection of copper ion was achievable by using as-prepared GCDs@RSPN nanoprobe. The nanohybrid probe has been successfully applied for copper ion detection in real water samples. Furthermore, the GCDs@RSPN ratiometric probe has also been applied to fabricate paper-based nanosensors for the portable detection of Cu2+.

Sunset yellow (SY) was purchased from Shanghai Maikun Chemical Co., Ltd. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), calcium chloride (CaCl2), barium chloride (BaCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2)) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Tetrahydrofuran (THF, anhydrous, ≥99.9%, stabilized by BHT) and ferric chloride (FeCl3) were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co. Ltd. Polyfluorene-4,7-dithiophene-2,1,3-Benzothiadiazole (PFDBT-5) was obtained as a gift from Professor Changfeng Wu from Southern University of Science and Technology. All other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise stated, all reagents used were of analytical grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared by using ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ•cm).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded with LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and UV–vis absorption spectra were captured by Lambda 35 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The fluorescence lifetimes were determined by FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). HT-7700 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan) was used to characterize the morphology of GCDs, RSPN and GCDs@RSPN. ZS90 nanometer particle size potential analyzer (Malvern, UK) was used to obtain the particle size distributions and Zeta potentials of GCDs, RSPN and GCDs@RSPN. FTIR spectra were collected with a INVENIOS Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Powder X-ray diffractometry (PXRD) PXRD patterns were obtained by a Bruker AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). A smartphone with an RGB color reader application (app) was used for image processing to obtain RGB information.

In detail, 0.04 g of SY and 0.40 g of PEI were co-dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave chamber. The mixture was reacted at 120 °C for 8 h and cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, a 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membrane was used to remove large aggregate particles, and then the solution was transferred to a dialysis bag (MWCO = 12000–14000 Da). After 36 h of dialysis, a green fluorescent GCDs solution was obtained. Finally, the resultant GCDs were freeze-dried and stored away from light.

The RSPN were prepared according to the nanoprecipitation method reported in the relevant literature [27]. Briefly, the polymer PFDBT-5 was dissolved in anhydrous tetrahydrofuran (THF) to prepare a stock solution with a mass concentration of 1 mg mL−1. A homogeneous THF solution (5 mL) containing 50 µg mL−1 PFDBT-5 was quickly injected into 10 mL of ultrapure water under vigorous ultrasound and the ultrasound was continued for another 8 min. The resulting solution was transformed into a 50 mL round-bottom flask, and the THF was removed by reduced pressure distillation at 55 °C. Then, the above solution was condensed to 5 mL total volume, and filtered with a 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membrane to remove small amount of large particles. The obtained RSPN were clear with the concentration of 50 µg mL−1 and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The GCDs@RSPN nanohybrids were prepared through electrostatic interaction. Briefly, 4.7 mL of RSPN (50 µg mL−1) was mixed with 300 µL of GCDs (25 mg mL−1), and the pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 7.0. After shaking for 4 h, the GCDs@RSPN nanohybrids were purified by ultrafiltration (Millipore ® Ultra-4 centrifugal filter device, MWCO: 100 KD, rotation speed: 3000 rpm) to remove the free GCDs. Then the final product was re-diluted to 5 mL with ultrapure water to obtain the desired GCDs@RSPN complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for fluorescence detection of Cu2+.
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 80 µL of GCDs@RSPN (47 µg mL−1) and different concentrations of Cu2+ (0–200 µM) were mixed, and diluted to 800 µL with phosphate buffer (PB, 50 mM, pH = 8.0). After shaking for 10 min, fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 400–720 nm with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the real water samples.
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples were obtained from the laboratory tap water, the Jinghu Lake near the school and the Yangtze River. These water samples were filtered by 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membrane to remove large particles, and then stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use. For the determination of Cu2+ in real water samples, the following detection experiment was consistent with the above detection procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper-based sensor for visual detection of Cu2+.
ce:section.ce:para: A cellulose chromatography paper was cut into several small square-shaped filter paper pieces. Then, the above small filter papers were evenly dropped in the GCDs@RSPN aqueous solution and air dried naturally. For the visual detection of Cu2+, a series of Cu2+ solutions with different concentrations were drip-coated on the probe-loaded filter paper. After natural air drying, these indicating papers were placed in a dark box and the fluorescence images were captured with irradiation at 365 nm. The color recognizer applet installed in the smartphone was used to fetch the color informations (RGB values) from those fluorescence images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategy of the ratiometric probe.
ce:section.ce:para: The designing strategy of GCDs@RSPN probe for Cu2+ detection was shown in Scheme 1. In this system, green fluorescence GCDs centered at 501 nm, prepared via the hydrothermal reaction between sunset yellow and PEI, were firstly selected as signal indicator. First of all, the emission intensity of as-prepared GCDs can be quenched obviously in presence of Cu2+. In addition, previous studies have shown that CDs usually have a small size and uniform structure, affording the pre-condition for further assembly of composite materials. On the basis above, the RSPN derived from red fluorescent PFBT5 polymer were employed as the reference signal. Firstly, bare SPNs exhibit excellent stability (no aggregation and quenching) toward various ions [28]. In addition, the RSPN not only show large spectral separation from GCDs, but also have ultra large specific surface area originating from the unique clew-like nanostructure, which is very favorable to further assembly with small size nanoparticles. The green fluorescence GCDs were loaded on the surface of RSPN, leading to the formation of the GCDs@RSPN complex. The different fluorescence responses of GCDs and RSPN to Cu2+ can cause the change of relative intensity, thereby realizing the ratiometric fluorescence response to Cu2+.

To realize the above design, GCDs were synthesized using SY and PEI as raw materials through the one-pot hydrothermal method. The prepared GCDs were obtained as brown and sticky block solid (Fig. S1A), which emits bright green fluorescence under UV light irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. S1B). As shown in Fig. S4A, the GCDs colloidal aqueous solution exhibits a strong emission band centered at 501 nm. TEM and DLS characterization show that the synthesized GCDs are well dispersed with the average hydrodynamic diameter of 3.15 nm (Fig. 1A). High-resolution TEM image reveals that the lattice spacing of GCDs (0.250 nm) is close to the (100) lattice spacing of graphite (inset of Fig. 1A) [29]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum displays a broad diffraction peak centered at 20° (Fig. 1B), suggesting that the GCDs are highly disordered carbon atoms. As depicted in Fig. 1C, FTIR spectrum shows the typical bands of stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H at 3314 cm−1, C–H at 2962 cm−1 and 2831 cm−1, C = O/COOH at 1645 cm−1, N–H at 1572 cm−1, C-N at 1409 cm−1, C–H at 1310 cm-1and S = O/C-O at 1093 cm−1, suggesting that there are a large number of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on the surface of GCDs. Zeta potentials of GCDs were measured to be + 22.20 mV (Fig. 1F). In addition, the physical and chemical properties of the synthesized GCDs have also been investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of GCDs remains almost constant over 150 min with 380 nm excitation, representing the excellent photostability of GCDs. Fig. S3 shows that no obvious changes of fluorescence emission intensity are observed even if the ionic strength is up to 4000 mM, suggesting satisfying ionic strength resistance. Taken together, the as-prepared GCDs, with many satisfying features, are very conducive to construct hybrid nanoprobe for chemical analysis and biosensing applications.

The RSPN featuring unique clew-like nanostructure were prepared by typical nanoprecipitation method. TEM and DLS results display that the as-prepared RSPN have uniform morphology and excellent monodispersity with the average hydrodynamic diameter of 31.10 nm (Fig. 1D). Zeta potential of RSPNs was measured to be −21.97 mV (Fig. 1F), mainly deriving from the partially polymer oxidation during RSPN formation, which is supported by previous report [30]. The GCDs@RSPN composite system was fabricated by mixing green fluorescent GCDs and red emission RSPN. Due to the opposite surface charge, green fluorescent GCDs were easily loaded on the surface of RSPN by electrostatic adsorption. After removing the free GCDs, the GCDs@RSPN had Zeta potentials of + 60.07 mV, confirming the above electrostatic assembly. As shown in Fig. 1E, it can be seen that GCDs are tightly attached to the surface of RSPN. The average hydrodynamic diameter of GCDs@RSPN was measured to be 71.39 nm. Taken together, the GCDs@RSPN composite system was successfully prepared.

Absorption peaks of GCDs are located at 245 nm and 385 nm, respectively, and that of RSPN is located at 380 nm and 511 nm, respectively (Fig. S4B). Considering being excited simultaneously, 380 nm was employed as the excitation wavelength for GCDs@RSPN composite. In addition, the weak absorption of RSPN at 511 nm partially overlaps with the emission peak of GCDs, providing the basis for available intra-particle energy transfer in GCDs@RSPN nanohybrids. The optical properties of GCDs@RSPN composites are shown in Fig. S4A. Taken together, the obtained nanohybrids exhibit two well-resolved emission peaks located at 501 nm and 625 nm, which derive from GCDs and RSPN, respectively. The large peak separation is beneficial for constructing ratiometric sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of the fluorescence of GCDs@RSPN to Cu2+.
ce:section.ce:para:
The as-prepared GCDs@RSPN probes show sensitive ratiometric response to Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 2
A, upon excitation at 380 nm, the intensity of green emission band decreases gradually, while that of red emission shows only a negligible decline with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. The large ratiometric value variation resulted in a distinct fluorescence color change of the solution under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 2B), indicating that visual detection of Cu2+ by the naked eye can be achieved. A good semi-logarithmic correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I501/I625) and the concentration of Cu2+ was observed in the range from 0 to 100 μM, and the correlation coefficient was R2 = 0.996 (Fig. 2C). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.577 μM (S/N = 3), which is lower than that of 0.953 μM using single fluorescence quenching GCDs probe (Fig. 2F), suggesting that higher sensitivity can be achieved by using the ratiometric probe. As shown in Table S1, compared with other Cu2+ sensing platforms, GCDs@RSPN probe has a wider detection range and lower detection limit, and achieves ratiometric quantitative detection of Cu2+. The selectivity of GCDs@RSPN was evaluated by monitoring the relative intensity (I 501/I 625) change in presence of various metal ions (Na+, K+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Zr4+, Cd2+, Pb2+ Ba2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Co2+) and small molecules substances (DL-valine, sarcosine, L-proline, citrulline, glucose, threonine, L-arginine). As shown in Fig. 2D, Fe3+, Ni2+ and Co2+ also can cause fluorescence response to this system, and these responses can be eliminated with the aid of EDTA. Therefore, this proposed GCDs@RSPN has satisfied selectivity and can be applied for determination of Cu2+.

The sensing mechanism of the ratiometric GCDs@RSPN probe was discussed. As mentioned above, upon addition of Cu2+, the green fluorescence derived from GCDs is continuously decreased, while red emission originating from RSPN decrease slightly, suggesting that the ratiometric response of GCDs@RSPN to Cu2+ mainly comes from the fluorescence quenching of GCDs. To reveal Cu2+-induced the fluorescence quenching of GCDs, the fluorescence lifetime of GCDs and GCDs + Cu2+ were measured. As shown in Fig. 3
A, the fluorescence lifetime of GCDs decreases from 4.461 ns to 1.671 ns after adding Cu2+, indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of GCDs is dynamic quenching. The coordination between the surface groups of GCDs and metal ions is the basis for photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [31]. According to reported literature, luminescent materials, synthesized from PEI precursors, can respond to Cu2+ by forming copper ammonia complex [32,33], leading to fluorescence quenching. In addition, absorption spectra were also used to study the above quenching mechanism. From Fig. 3B, it can be observed that green fluorescent GCDs display two absorption peaks located near 245 nm and 385 nm (black curve a), respectively. Upon addition of Cu2+, two new UV–Vis absorption bands were found, one at 265 nm and the other at 500–850 nm (red curve b), which is coincide well with previously published [33]. So it is speculated that these new absorption bands can be attributed to the copper amine complex formed between surface amino groups of GCDs and Cu2+. The GCDs, featuring abundant amino groups, have large amount of lone-pair electrons and can be used as electron donors. The photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from GCDs to Cu2+ is feasible, leading to the fluorescence quenching of GCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for detection in real samples.
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the practicability of the ratiometric probe in actual samples, the concentrations of Cu2+ in tap water from the laboratory, water from Jinghu Lake near the school, and water from the Yangtze River were measured. As presented in Table S2-S4, the recoveries were obtained in the range of 100.4%-105.8%, 95.7%-101.3%, and 99.0%-101.5%, respectively. These results indicate that GCDs@RSPN has the potential for Cu2+ detection in actual samples. Additionally, the test strips prepared by the GCDs@RSPN probe were used for the portable detection of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 4A, the test strips loaded with GCDs@RSPN probe show coral color under a 365 nm UV lamp. When the concentrations of the copper solution dropped on the strip increase from 0 μM to 200 μM, the color of the test strips gradually darken from coral, and finally turn to dark pink. The RGB value of test strips were captured through a smartphone application (color recognizer) and calculated the intensity ratio of the green channel to the red channel (G/R). Fig. 4B shows that G/R has a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.936) with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 5–80 μM. The above results show that paper-based sensor can simplify the detection process by combining the function of the color recognizer in the smartphone, and realize the semi-quantitative analysis of Cu2+ detection on site.

In summary, we have developed a nanohybrid probe for ratiometric Cu2+ detection. The nanohybrids GCDs@RSPN were fabricated between GCDs and RSPN via electrostatic interaction. The probe exhibited many advantages, including simple preparation, excellent water solubility, high sensitivity and wide linear response range. Then, obtained GCDs@RSPN probe has been successfully applied to detect the Cu2+ in actual samples. Furthermore, the portable smartphone-assisted platform has been fabricated and successfully applied for the visual detection of copper iron, affording a promising tool for environmental analysis.

Fabao Luo: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Mengjun Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Yizhang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Junyong Sun: Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Feng Gao: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 22274003, 21874001, 21605001), the program for top talent of Universities in Anhui Province (gxbjZD81), the Natural Science Foundation of University of Anhui Province(KJ2020A0100, 2022AH052120), and the Foundation for Innovation Team of Bioanalytical Chemistry of Anhui Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122574.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots based multicolor fluorescence sensor for ratiometric and colorimetric dual-model detection of Cu2+
Dyes Pigm.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Huang, X. Luo, W. Lin, W. Tang, T. Yue, J. Wang, et al., Carbon dots based multicolor fluorescence sensor for ratiometric and colorimetric dual-model detection of Cu2+, Dyes and Pigments, 203(2022) 110381, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The molecular mechanisms of copper metabolism and its roles in human diseases, Pflugers Archiv-European
J. Physiol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chen, Y. Jiang, H. Shi, Y. Peng, X. Fan, C. Li, The molecular mechanisms of copper metabolism and its roles in human diseases, Pflugers Archiv-European Journal of Physiology, 472(2020) 1415-1429, https://doi.org/10.1007/s00424-020-02412-2.
